title: Container Threat Models, Users, Capabilities, Seccomp, Rootless, and Daemon Authority · Operations Transfer
summary: An independent delivery team receives unfamiliar application constraints and must transfer container threat models, users, capabilities, seccomp, rootless, and daemon authority into a new least-privilege container threat model and hardening profile without copying the workshop scenario. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of least-privilege container threat model and hardening profile with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explic…
objectives: Model untrusted image, build, registry, daemon, runtime, kernel, mount, device, network, and supply-chain threats without treating containers as sandboxes by default.
Run as a deliberate non-root UID/GID and reconcile image ownership, mounted files, rootless mappings, and required privileged startup work.
Start from dropped capabilities and add only measured needs while treating privileged, devices, host namespaces, and Docker socket access as host authority.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# docker-basics cumulative container delivery portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never connect to a Docker daemon, mount a socket, or execute learner commands.
workspace: docker
version-baseline:
  engine: 29.6.1
  compose: 5.3.1
  buildx: 0.35.0
  buildkit: 0.31.1
  dockerfile-frontend: 1.25
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-environment
evidence:

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Lab Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel
steps.instruction:
lab case — A lab document parser runs privileged as root, mounts daemon authority, writes the image filesystem, has network access, and no resource caps. Remove authority and state the remaining shared-kernel risk. Competency probe: Model untrusted image, build, registry, daemon, runtime, kernel, mount, device, network, and supply-chain threats without treating containers as sandboxes by default.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Lab Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for model untrusted image, build, registry, daemon, runtime, kernel, mount, device, network, and supply-chain threats without treating containers as sandboxes by default. For Commit to the mission model · Lab Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel, keep Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel connected to mechanically reviewable Docker artifact and transfer evidence.

content.text: A lab document parser runs privileged as root, mounts daemon authority, writes the image filesystem, has network access, and no resource caps. Remove authority and state the remaining shared-kernel risk. Competency probe: Model untrusted image, build, registry, daemon, runtime, kernel, mount, device, network, and supply-chain threats without treating containers as sandboxes by default.

content.title: Commit before feedback
content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel condition decided your prediction.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: Docker Engine Security, Rootless, Seccomp, and API Protection — Controls daemon authority, rootless and user namespaces, users, capabilities, seccomp, mandatory access control, secrets, privileged boundaries, and socket protection.

hints: For Commit to the mission model · Lab Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel, name the daemon authority, immutable identity, process, storage, network, secret, and cleanup boundary, Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Commit to the mission model · Lab Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel, trace this concrete input and predict build, image, container, mount, network, Compose, policy, and teardown evidence before reviewing it: A lab document parser runs privileged as root, mounts daemon authority, writes the image filesystem, has network access, and no resource caps. Remove authority and state the remaining shared-kernel risk. Competency probe: Model untrusted image, build, registry, daemon, runtime, kernel, mount, device, network, and supply-chain threats without treating containers as sandboxes by default.

For Commit to the mission model · Lab Docker Threat Boundary Host Kernel, challenge the documented misconception with manifest, inspect, lifecycle, failure, repair, and transfer evidence: Any process inside a container is safe to run because namespaces prevent host impact.

options.text: For commit to the mission model, copy one familiar Docker command, omit daemon authority, immutable identity, failure, cleanup, and disposable-environment transfer checks, then accept a successful build as production proof.

steps.title: Audit the initial evidence packet · Lab Docker Rootless Userns Daemon Socket

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab Docker Rootless Userns Daemon Socket
steps.instruction:
Append the Docker evidence item headed "  # Evidence: docker-rootless-userns-daemon-socket-qah9gv". Tailor its security-policy.yaml change to the current stakeholder case, retain a deterministic review command, reject one concrete failure, and record a repair. The browser analyzes text only; run Docker commands later in an authorized disposable transfer environment.

Evidence target: Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab Docker Rootless Userns Daemon Socket.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Docker Rootless Userns Daemon Socket into observable performance evidence. For Extend the cumulative artifact · Lab Docker Rootless Userns Daemon Socket, keep Docker Rootless Userns Daemon Socket connected to mechanically reviewable Docker artifact and transfer evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: docker-rootless-userns-daemon-socket-qah9gv
  - competency: docker-rootless-userns-daemon-socket
    artifact: security-policy.yaml
    decision: rootless userns daemon socket
    scenario-variant: 3
    environment: remote context crossing a separately authorized daemon boundary
    fault-injection: restore a volume under a different numeric user identity
    retained-proof: accessible recovery runbook with rollback rehearsal timing
    case-key: qah9gv
    change: |
      user: 10001:10001
      read_only: true
      cap_drop: [ALL]
      security_opt: [no-new-privileges:true]
      tmpfs: [/tmp]
      network_mode: none
    verify: docker inspect document-worker --format json
    failure: Reject privileged mode, daemon-socket mounting, broad devices, root-by-default, or a claim that containers form a hostile-code sandbox.
    repair: Remove daemon authority, choose rootless or user namespaces where applicable, drop capabilities, bound writable paths, and state residual kernel risk.
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.
